mnemonics.title: ARGc = ARGument Count
mnemonics.content: REMEMBER: argc stands for ARGument COUNT - it counts the number of arguments including the program name. ALWAYS VALIDATE argc before using argv!

mnemonics.title: ARGV = ARgument Vector
mnemonics.content: argv is an ARRAY (vector) of character pointers, each pointing to a string. Think of it as an array of words.

mnemonics.title: argv[0] = NAME
mnemonics.content: argv[0] = NAME of the program. Just remember: ZERO is for the NAME!

mnemonics.title: argv[argc] = NULL
mnemonics.content: argv[argc] is ALWAYS NULL - the sentinel at the end tells you where the array stops.

mnemonics.title: ATOI = ASCII TO Integer
mnemonics.content: Remember ATOI as 'ASCII TO Integer' - it converts string digits to integer values.

mnemonics.title: CHECK BEFORE ACCESS
mnemonics.content: Always CHECK argc BEFORE ACCESSing argv. Think: 'If argc < expected + 1, show usage and exit!'

acronyms.expansion: ARGument Count - the number of arguments passed to the program
acronyms.usage: Used to determine how many arguments were provided at runtime

acronyms.expansion: ARGument Vector - array of pointers to argument strings
acronyms.usage: Access individual command line arguments using argv[1], argv[2], etc.

acronyms.expansion: ASCII TO Integer - converts string to integer
ASCII TO Float - converts string to floating-point
STRing TO Long - converts with error checking
acronyms.usage: strtol("123", &endptr, 10) converts with base 10 and error detection

visualTips.visualization: Draw a box with slots 0, 1, 2, 3... Each slot contains a string. The number above shows argc = 4. Last slot (4) shows NULL.
Think of argv as a row of pointers (arrows), each pointing to a separate string stored somewhere in memory. The array itself only holds the addresses.
Draw a number line from 0 to argc-1. Highlight that only indices 0 to (argc-1) are valid. Anything beyond is 'danger zone' (undefined behavior).

analogies.concept: Command line arguments as restaurant order
analogies.analogy: Think of a restaurant: argc is like the count of items ordered (including the dish itself), and argv is like the list of items (with the first being 'restaurant name' = program name).
argv is like a row of hotel room keys (pointers), each key (pointer) opens a different room (string). Room 0 is always the hotel lobby (program name).

analogies.concept: argc validation as guest count check
analogies.analogy: Checking argc before accessing argv is like checking how many guests arrived before handing out name tags - you don't want to give tags to non-existent guests!
